title: Origin Fairness, Politeness, Retries, Backoff, and p-limit · Contract Defense
summary: A release reviewer compares near-miss TypeScript decisions for origin fairness, politeness, retries, backoff, and p-limit and accepts only compiler plus runtime changed-case evidence, explicit non-claims, and named network, DNS, browser, accessibility, security, load, legal-review, and production transfer gates. Planned artifact: Record independent decisions and explanations for publisher-respecting origin scheduler. Learners progress through prediction, explicit theory, changed-case practice,…
objectives: Maintain per-origin concurrency, minimum spacing, next-eligible time, request and byte budgets, and owner overrides.
Retry only classified transient and idempotent cases with attempt, elapsed, and owner budgets.
Parse valid Retry-After, combine it with capped exponential backoff and bounded jitter, and record the chosen delay.
Preserve previously learned constraints and attach observable changed-case evidence.

steps.title: Authorized site auditing and TypeScript Origin Buckets · changed case · Quiz Origin Buckets
steps.instruction:
quiz case — A quiz TypeScript team handles case 8898 during a narrow-screen keyboard and screen-reader review. Build deterministic TypeScript evidence to prove per-origin eligibility, retry classification, Retry-After, bounded jitter, p-limit boundaries, and clock control; every Promise, stream, listener, timer, socket, file, browser context, and report sink needs one cleanup owner; then change one owner permission, seed, URL, robots, sitemap, redirect, DNS, media, or encoding decision and reconcile provenance. Browser TypeScript uses pure bounded models and original URL, robots, sitemap, HTTP, HTML, frontier, accessibility, browser, and report fixtures only. Real DNS, Fetch, streams, Cheerio, Playwright, axe-core, files, processes, packages, containers, assistive-technology review, load, legal review, restore, and production behavior require explicit controlled transfer gates.
